one_line_takeaway: Steering an LVLM watermark toward visually-grounded tokens (via a prefix-tuned evidence extractor plus uncertainty-gated green-list swapping) preserves image fidelity and reduces hallucination while keeping detection AUC near state-of-the-art and robust to text-space attacks.
key_finding: VISA-Mark improves visual fidelity and text quality without sacrificing watermark detectability. On the LLaVA backbone / MS-COCO 2014, VISA-Mark attains Chair-I 16.39 (lower = fewer hallucinations) versus KGW 17.37, VLA 17.94, and SWEET 19.25, alongside the top AUC 97.95 (vs KGW 95.70, SWEET 96.50, VLA 89.29), best PPL 5.52 and BertScore 93.07. The abstract summarizes this as a 7.8% Chair-I improvement in visual consistency, ~96.88% AUC detection, and ~99.3% attack-resilience. Under text-space attacks (5% tokens modified), VISA-Mark's detection AUC is 99.69% with no attack and degrades only slightly to 98.97% (insertion), 99.27% (deletion), and 99.59% (synonym substitution), whereas baselines DiP and Unbiased collapse by roughly 7% into the 88–91% range.
core_contribution: The paper proposes VISA-Mark (VIsual Semantic Adaptive Watermark), a decoding-time watermarking framework for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that makes watermark injection vision-adaptive rather than vision-agnostic. Its own three-part contribution is: (A) a Visual Evidence Extractor, a lightweight prefix-tuner (built on prefix-tuning, which the paper cites as ref [45], trained offline) that lets a frozen LVLM estimate per-token "Visual Evidence Weights" quantifying how well each candidate token is supported by the image; (B) Uncertainty-based Vocabulary Partitioning, which swaps high-evidence tokens into a fixed-ratio green list during low-uncertainty (low-entropy) steps to avoid randomly excluding visually critical tokens; and (C) Evidence-Calibrated Logit Perturbation, which scales the logit bias by the visual evidence weight so watermark strength concentrates on visually-grounded tokens. The stated goal is to embed a detectable statistical watermark for content traceability/IP protection while preserving visual fidelity (reducing hallucination) and inference efficiency. Note: KGW (Kirchenbauer et al., green-list vocabulary partitioning) is cited prior work and a baseline, NOT this paper's method; likewise SWEET, Unbiased, DiP, and VLA-Mark are cited baselines.
threat_model: Defensive watermarking for provenance/traceability of LVLM-generated text. Defender: an LVLM provider that embeds an imperceptible, statistically detectable signal into generated captions/text so outputs can later be attributed and misuse (misinformation, IP infringement) traced. Adversary: a party who obtains watermarked outputs and attempts to evade detection via meaning-preserving text-space edits (random word insertion, deletion, synonym substitution at ~5% tokens); the watermark must survive these. The paper explicitly notes it does NOT defend against adaptive attacks that specifically target the visual-evidence-extraction mechanism, listing that as future work.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) the prefix-tuner is trained on dense-caption data, so generalization to out-of-distribution domains (e.g., medical imaging, abstract art) may degrade without domain adaptation; (2) robustness is demonstrated only against common text-space attacks — vulnerability to adaptive attacks aimed at the evidence-extraction module is untested; (3) evidence is extracted primarily from noun phrases / object-level entities, so fine-grained attribute and relational inconsistencies are not addressed. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: gains in Chair-I/AUC over the strongest baselines are modest in absolute terms (e.g., Chair-I 16.39 vs KGW 17.37), evaluation is limited to two models and captioning-style benchmarks, and claimed inference-efficiency preservation is asserted but the paper I read does not quantify latency in the main results.

datasets_used: DCI (used as external knowledge for prefix training)
quantitative_results: LLaVA / MS-COCO 2014: VISA-Mark AUC 97.95, PPL 5.52, BertScore 93.07, Chair-I 16.39
Chair-I baselines (LLaVA/COCO14): KGW 17.37, VLA 17.94, SWEET 19.25, DiP 16.91, Unbiased 17.57
AUC baselines (LLaVA/COCO14): KGW 95.70, SWEET 96.50, VLA 89.29, DiP 84.37, Unbiased 84.33
Abstract headline: 7.8% Chair-I improvement in visual consistency, 96.88% AUC, 99.3% attack resilience
Robustness AUC: 99.69% no-attack, 98.97% word-insertion, 99.27% word-deletion, 99.59% synonym-substitution (5% token modification)
Baseline degradation under attack: DiP and Unbiased drop ~7% AUC into 88-91% range
Ablation: optimal hyperparameter setting maintains AUC ~94-96% while minimizing hallucination (Table 2)
